chunks.text: A socially designed experiment revealed something about human nature.
Someone would " lose " a wallet and a stranger would find it.
The experiment was to assess whether the wallet came back.
The result was astonishing.
Tested on street corners in more than forty countries, the more cash the wallet contained
the higher the percentage of return.
At first glance this seems odd.
With more money, the temptation to keep it should be greater.
But humans typically strongly dislike thinking of themselves as a thief.
The behavior of taking the money upsets one's self - image.
The greater the amount, the greater the psychological cost.
So, on the counter, the rate of return rises.
This experiment suggests that humans are essentially good.
It fundamentally challenges the notion, long presumed by economists
that people act only out of self - interest.
Of course, there are exceptions.
Crime is not zero.
But overall, the majority of people are honest and wish to help others.
This fact brings us relief.
The wrongdoing we see in the news stands out
but the quiet goodness of daily life supports the world.
Trust in humanity rests on a positive reality.
